result:
**On the Strange Joy of Getting Lost**

I've been thinking lately about the art of getting lost—not metaphorically, though there's plenty to say about that too, but literally, physically lost. It seems to me that we've engineered this particular human experience almost entirely out of existence, and I'm not sure we've fully reckoned with what we've given up in the exchange.

Last month, I deliberately left my phone at home and went for a walk in a part of the city I didn't know well. No GPS, no ability to quickly look up where I was, no blue dot pulsing on a screen to tell me my exact coordinates on Earth. Just me, the streets, and a vague sense that I should probably head generally eastward if I wanted to get home before dark. The experience was simultaneously anxiety-inducing and oddly liberating, like remembering how to use a muscle I'd forgotten I had.

There's something primordial about being lost. For most of human history, it was a genuine danger—losing your way could mean death from exposure, starvation, or predation. Our ancestors who survived were the ones who paid careful attention to landmarks, who could read the sun and stars, who remembered the shape of the mountain ridge that meant home was near. Getting lost meant engaging every sense, every bit of intelligence and intuition you possessed, in the urgent task of finding your way back.

Now, of course, we're never really lost. We carry our exact position in our pockets, accessible with a swipe. We can summon a car to our precise location with a few taps. We can share our coordinates with friends, drop pins, save locations, retrace our steps through the digital breadcrumbs our phones leave behind. This is undeniably useful—I'm not romanticizing the genuine terror of being truly lost in dangerous circumstances. But I do think something subtle and important has shifted in our relationship with space and place.

When you can't rely on GPS, you pay attention differently. On that phone-free walk, I found myself noticing things I normally would have scrolled past: the way certain trees leaned over the sidewalk creating natural arches, the peculiar blue-green of a house's shutters, the smell of bread from a bakery I didn't know existed, the sound of wind chimes in someone's backyard. These became my navigational aids, my landmarks. I was creating a mental map in real-time, the kind our brains evolved to make over millions of years.

There's neuroscience behind this. The hippocampus, that seahorse-shaped structure deep in our brains, is crucial for both memory formation and spatial navigation. Studies of London taxi drivers, who must memorize the city's entire street layout, show they have larger posterior hippocampi than average. But what happens when we outsource this navigation to our devices? Some researchers worry we're literally reshaping our brains, potentially weakening our spatial memory and our ability to create cognitive maps of our environment.

But beyond the neurological implications, I think there's something else at stake: the possibility of genuine surprise. When you're following turn-by-turn directions, you arrive exactly where you intended to go. Efficient? Yes. But efficiency isn't everything. Some of my most cherished discoveries have come from being lost: a tiny restaurant tucked into an alley I'd never have found otherwise, a pocket park with the most extraordinary oak tree, a used bookstore that seemed to exist in its own temporal dimension, somehow outside the normal flow of the 21st century.

Getting lost also changes your relationship with other people. When you're genuinely unsure where you are, you have to ask for help. This might seem like a small thing, but in our increasingly frictionless, appless world, how often do we really need to approach a stranger and ask for something? There's vulnerability in admitting you don't know where you are, and generosity in the person who stops to help. Some of my most pleasant interactions with strangers have begun with "Excuse me, I'm a bit lost—could you tell me how to get to...?"

I remember being lost in Tokyo before smartphones were ubiquitous, unable to read the street signs, trying to find my way back to my hotel. An elderly man not only gave me directions but walked me several blocks out of his way to make sure I found the right subway entrance. We couldn't communicate much—my Japanese was nonexistent, his English minimal—but his kindness transcended language. Would that interaction have happened if I'd had Google Maps? Would I remember it so vividly nearly two decades later?

There's also something to be said for the stories that come from being lost. "I took a wrong turn and ended up..." is the beginning of so many adventures. Literature is full of characters who get lost and find something—themselves, love, wisdom, home. Dante's Divine Comedy begins with the narrator lost in a dark wood. Alice falls down a rabbit hole. Odysseus spends ten years lost on his way home from Troy. Would any of these stories resonate if the characters could have just pulled out their phones and recalculated the route?

Of course, I recognize the privilege in being able to choose to get lost. For many people throughout history and today, being lost isn't an adventure but a crisis. Refugees fleeing violence, migrants crossing dangerous borders, homeless individuals navigating hostile cities—for them, being lost can be life-threatening. The luxury of deliberately getting lost for the experience of it depends on knowing you have a safe home to return to, resources to fall back on, a social safety net if things go really wrong.

And yet, within the bounds of safety and reason, I think there's value in occasionally disconnecting from our technological tethers and experiencing the mild disorientation of not quite knowing where we are. It reminds us that the world is bigger than our routines, that our mental maps are always incomplete, that there are still discoveries to be made even in familiar places.

Children understand this intuitively. Watch a child in a new place—they don't efficiently navigate from point A to point B. They meander, explore, double back, investigate. They climb things to see what's on the other side, peek around corners, follow interesting sounds. They get lost in the best possible way, absorbed in the immediacy of their surroundings. We train this out of them, of course, teaching them to stay close, follow the path, don't wander off. This is necessary for their safety, but something is lost in the bargain.

I've been trying to cultivate what you might call "productive lostness"—putting myself in situations where I don't quite know what's going to happen or where exactly I am. This doesn't mean being reckless; it means building in space for uncertainty. Taking a different route home from work. Exploring a new neighborhood without researching it first. Following interesting side streets just to see where they go.

The poet Rebecca Solnit writes about this beautifully in "A Field Guide to Getting Lost." She distinguishes between losing things and becoming lost, arguing that the latter can be a deliberate and valuable practice. "Lost really has two disparate meanings," she writes. "Losing things is about the familiar falling away, getting lost is about the unfamiliar appearing." When we allow ourselves to get lost, we open ourselves to new possibilities, new perspectives, new ways of being in the world.

This extends beyond physical navigation. We can get lost in books, in music, in conversation, in thought. These experiences of absorption, where we lose track of time and our sense of self dissolves into something larger, are increasingly rare in our hyperconnected, notification-driven world. Every ping, buzz, and banner pulls us back to the mapped, known, quantified world. We know exactly where we are at all times—not just physically but socially, professionally, economically. We can check our position relative to others with a few swipes. We know how many steps we've taken, how many calories we've burned, how many minutes until our next meeting.

But what if we didn't know? What if we allowed ourselves periods of not knowing? There's a particular quality of attention that emerges when you're slightly lost, a heightened awareness that's different from our usual goal-directed consciousness. You become more present, more observant, more alive to possibility. You're not following a predetermined path; you're creating the path as you go.

I think about the phrase "finding yourself" and how it implies you were lost to begin with. Maybe that's not wrong. Maybe we need to get lost periodically to remember who we are when we're not following directions, meeting expectations, fulfilling roles. Maybe our truest selves emerge not when we know exactly where we're going, but when we don't.

The technology writer Nicholas Carr worries that our reliance on GPS and similar technologies is creating a world of "automation complacency," where we become passive consumers of directions rather than active navigators of space. He cites studies showing that people who use GPS to navigate show less activity in the hippocampus and have worse spatial memory than those who navigate by conventional means. We're outsourcing not just the work of navigation but the cognitive benefits that come with it.

This isn't just about nostalgia for paper maps, though I do have fond memories of spreading them out on the hood of the car, tracing routes with my finger, arguing about the best way to go. It's about maintaining a direct, unmediated relationship with the physical world. When we're constantly consulting our phones for directions, we're living in a hybrid space—partly in the physical world, partly in the digital representation of it. We're looking at the map more than the territory.

But the territory is where life actually happens. The map, no matter how detailed, is always an abstraction, a reduction. It can tell you where the coffee shop is, but not that the barista has a beautiful smile or that they play Coltrane on Sunday mornings. It can show you the fastest route, but not the one that takes you past the garden where the roses are in spectacular bloom.

I'm not suggesting we abandon GPS entirely—that would be impractical and, in many cases, unsafe. But I am suggesting we might benefit from occasionally choosing to get lost, or at least to navigate by our own wits and senses. Turn off the voice directions and figure it out yourself. Take the paper map instead of the phone. Ask a local for recommendations instead of consulting Yelp.

There's a meditation practice called "aimless wandering" where you walk without any destination in mind, letting your feet choose the path. Each intersection becomes a choice made not by logic or efficiency but by intuition or simple curiosity. Where does this lead? What's around that corner? The practice is meant to break us out of our goal-oriented mindset and reconnect us with spontaneous presence.

I've started doing this in small ways. When I have extra time, I'll get off the subway a stop early and walk the rest of the way, deliberately taking streets I don't know. I'll go to a museum without checking the map, letting myself wander from room to room based on what catches my eye. I'll drive to a nearby town I've never visited and explore without an agenda.

These small adventures in lostness have reminded me that the world is still mysterious, still full of undiscovered places, even in my own backyard. They've taught me that efficiency isn't always the highest value, that the shortest distance between two points isn't always the most interesting route, that sometimes the best discoveries come from not knowing where you're going.

Getting lost, I've come to believe, is a skill we're in danger of losing. Like so many embodied skills—handwriting, mental arithmetic, remembering phone numbers—it's becoming vestigial, something our grandchildren might not even understand. "You mean you didn't know where you were? And you couldn't just check your phone?"

But I hope not. I hope we preserve some space for productive disorientation, for the fertile uncertainty of not quite knowing where we are. Because in those moments of lostness, when we can't rely on our devices to tell us where to go, we have to rely on ourselves—our senses, our intelligence, our courage, our willingness to ask for help. We have to be present, attentive, engaged. We have to be, in the fullest sense, alive to where we are.

The sun is setting now as I write this, painting the sky in shades of pink and gold I didn't expect. I know exactly where I am—at my desk, in my home, coordinates precisely locatable on any map. But in another sense, I'm lost in thought, wandering through ideas without a clear destination, following connections as they arise, not sure exactly where this essay would end up when I started it.

And that's perhaps the deepest value of getting lost: it reminds us that not everything needs to be mapped, planned, optimized. Some experiences are valuable precisely because they're inefficient, uncertain, surprising. In a world increasingly governed by algorithms designed to give us exactly what we want, getting lost offers us the gift of finding what we didn't know we were looking for.

So here's my modest proposal: get lost. Not forever, not dangerously, but deliberately and periodically. Turn off the GPS. Take the wrong exit. Wander. Meander. Explore. Let yourself not know, for a little while, exactly where you are. You might be surprised by what you find—including, perhaps, yourself.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
